perturbation.original_text: Company and BUYER mutually agree to hold the other party's Confidential Information in strict confidence and not to disclose such Confidential Information to any third parties except after receiving prior consent by the disclosing party in writing. Company and BUYER shall use the same degree of care to avoid disclosure of such Confidential Information as each employs with respect to its own proprietary information of like importance or a greater degree if reasonable.
perturbation.changed_text: Company and BUYER agree to use reasonable efforts to hold the other party's Confidential Information in confidence and may disclose such Confidential Information to third parties if deemed necessary. Company and BUYER shall use a degree of care to avoid disclosure of such Confidential Information that they consider adequate.
perturbation.explanation: The modification introduces ambiguity by replacing 'strict confidence' with 'reasonable efforts' and allowing disclosure to third parties if 'deemed necessary'. It also weakens the standard of care by using 'degree of care that they consider adequate,' making it subjective rather than objective.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA requires a showing of 'misappropriation,' which often involves a breach of duty to maintain secrecy. By allowing 'reasonable efforts' instead of 'strict confidence' and permitting disclosures deemed 'necessary,' it becomes more difficult to prove misappropriation under CUTSA, potentially weakening trade secret protection contrary to the intent of the law.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts trade secret law by weakening the duty to maintain secrecy. The original text requires 'strict confidence,' while the changed text only requires 'reasonable efforts' and allows disclosure if 'deemed necessary.' Snippet 1 defines 'improper means' as including 'breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy.' The change makes it harder to prove misappropriation because reasonable efforts and 'deemed necessary' disclosures are more difficult to construe as a breach of duty, thus creating a significant contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Company and BUYER agree that they will not use the other party's Confidential Information for any purpose other than for the intended purposes, without the prior written permission of the other party.
perturbation.changed_text: Company and BUYER agree that they should generally not use the other party's Confidential Information for purposes other than the intended purposes, unless an exception seems warranted.
perturbation.explanation: The change from 'will not use' to 'should generally not use' and the addition of 'unless an exception seems warranted' introduces significant ambiguity, undermining the obligation to protect confidential information. It creates a loophole where use of confidential information is permissible if a party subjectively believes an exception is warranted.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA protects against the unauthorized use of trade secrets. By introducing the caveat 'unless an exception seems warranted,' the modified text weakens the protection afforded by CUTSA, making it more difficult to prove misappropriation because the agreement now allows for exceptions to the non-use obligation at the discretion of the receiving party.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The original text provides a clear obligation to protect confidential information ('will not use'). The changed text weakens this obligation by using 'should generally not use' and introducing a subjective exception ('unless an exception seems warranted'). This introduces ambiguity and undermines the protection, potentially conflicting with the Uniform Trade Secrets Act, as the Law Explanation suggests CUTSA protects against unauthorized use of trade secrets. Scraped Law Snippet 1 cites the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CIV 3426), implying the contract's enforceability under this act is compromised by the added ambiguity.

perturbation.original_text: Company and BUYER mutually agree they may disclose such Confidential Information to their respective responsible employees with a bona fide need to know, and Company and BUYER agree to instruct all such employees not to disclose such Confidential Information to third parties and will ensure that such employees have agreed to similar non-disclosure provisions with Company or the BUYER, its own employees respectively.
perturbation.changed_text: Company and BUYER may generally disclose such Confidential Information to employees, and Company and BUYER will encourage employees not to disclose such Confidential Information to third parties.
perturbation.explanation: The modification replaces 'responsible employees with a bona fide need to know' with 'employees' (potentially broadening the scope of disclosure). It also weakens the obligation to instruct employees not to disclose confidential information by using the word 'encourage'. The requirement to ensure employees have agreed to non-disclosure provisions has been removed.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA requires that the trade secret holder take reasonable measures to protect the secrecy of the information. Weakening the restrictions on disclosure to employees and the removal of the requirement for employees to have agreed to non-disclosure provisions weakens the protection of the trade secret. Under CUTSA, this could potentially invalidate trade secret status because reasonable steps to maintain secrecy were not implemented.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the protection of confidential information, potentially violating CUTSA. The original text limited disclosure to 'responsible employees with a bona fide need to know' and required ensuring employees agreed to non-disclosure provisions. The changed text broadens disclosure to 'employees' generally and only 'encourages' non-disclosure, removing the assurance of a binding agreement. This contradicts the reasonable measures a trade secret holder must take to protect secrecy, as highlighted in the Law Explanation, and weakens the duty to maintain secrecy, as mentioned in California Civil Code 3426.1(a).

perturbation.original_text: All Confidential Information shall remain the property of the disclosing party, and upon the written request of either party, the other party shall promptly return to the disclosing party all Confidential Information disclosed to it and all copies thereof or at the disclosing party's option shall destroy all such Confidential Information and shall provide the receiving party with a certificate that all Confidential Information has been destroyed.
perturbation.changed_text: All Confidential Information should ideally remain the property of the disclosing party, and upon request the other party should consider returning the Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: The changes replace mandatory obligations with suggestions: 'shall remain' becomes 'should ideally remain' and 'shall promptly return' becomes 'should consider returning'. The obligation to destroy the confidential information and provide a certificate of destruction is removed.
perturbation.law_explanation: Under CUTSA, failing to retrieve or destroy confidential information after a relationship ends, when required by agreement, could be evidence of misappropriation if the information is subsequently used to the detriment of the disclosing party. The modified language makes the return of information discretionary, weakening the disclosing party's ability to enforce their rights and potentially increasing the risk of misappropriation.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandated the return or destruction of confidential information, but the modified version weakens this obligation by using suggestive language ('should ideally remain', 'should consider returning') and removing the destruction obligation. This creates an Ambiguities Legal Contradiction, since it makes the obligation difficult to enforce. While Scraped Law Snippet 2 is 'None', Scraped Law Snippet 1 references the Uniform Trade Secrets Act, indicating the importance of protecting trade secrets, something that is undermined by the modified text that allows for less stringent protection of confidential information.
